forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -0,0 +1,25 @@
+---
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: '8780'
+title: Add tests for CLIRegistryBuilder's emptyTool and duplicateOperation validation
+---
+Sources/OperationsCLI/CLIRegistryBuilder.swift:194-210
+
+Coverage: 90.6% (126/139 lines)
+
+Uncovered lines: 204 (`throw OperationCLIDriverError.emptyTool(tool.name)`) and 207 (the `.duplicateOperation` closure in `validateOperationsPerTool`):
+
+```swift
+private static func validateOperationsPerTool(_ tools: [AnyOperationTool]) throws {
+    for tool in tools {
+        guard !tool.operations.isEmpty else {
+            throw OperationCLIDriverError.emptyTool(tool.name)
+        }
+        try validateUnique(tool.operations.map(\.opString)) { opString in
+            .duplicateOperation(tool: tool.name, opString: opString)
+        }
+    }
+}
+```
+
+This is the "startup assertion pass" documented at the top of the file (duplicate names, malformed tree) — currently only `validateUniqueToolNames`'s duplicate-tool-name path is tested (per `OperationCLIDriverError.duplicateToolName` being covered elsewhere), but a tool with zero operations, and two operations in the same tool sharing an opString, are both untested. Add tests constructing an `OperationCLIDriver` with (a) a tool that has no operations, expecting `.emptyTool` to throw at init, and (b) a tool with two operations sharing the same verb/noun pair, expecting `.duplicateOperation` to throw at init.
\ No newline at end of file

 Sources/OperationsCLI/CLIRegistryBuilder.swift:194-210

forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -1,4 +1,18 @@
 ---
+comments:
+- actor: wballard
+  id: 01kwm0aayst2085p30zkbcr51z
+  text: |-
+    Investigated both scenarios before writing tests.
+
+    (b) duplicateOperation: straightforward — built an `OperationTool` with the same macro-generated operation type passed twice (`AnyOperation(AddNoteCLIFixture.self)` x2, both opString "add note"), then asserted `OperationCLIDriver(tool:)` throws `.duplicateOperation(tool: "notes", opString: "add note")`. Passes.
+
+    (a) emptyTool: NOT reachable via the natural `OperationTool<Context>` -> `AnyOperationTool` -> `OperationCLIDriver` path. Verified empirically: `OperationTool.init(operations: [])` itself throws first — `SchemaFusion.fuse` builds the `op` discriminator field via `DynamicGenerationSchema(anyOf: operations.map(\.opString))`, and an empty `anyOf` makes `GenerationSchema.SchemaError.emptyTypeChoices` throw before `CLIRegistryBuilder` (and its `.emptyTool` guard) is ever reached. So the natural construction path can never produce a zero-operations `AnyOperationTool` to feed into `CLIRegistryBuilder.build`.
+
+    Tried an extension-based fixture (`extension AnyOperationTool { init(emptyToolNamed:) { self.name = ...; operations = [] ... } }`) directly in the test file, relying on `AnyOperationTool`'s stored properties being `internal` (visible via `@testable import`). This does NOT compile — Swift disallows initializing another module's stored `let` properties directly from an extension outside that module, even under `@testable import` ("'let' property ... may not be initialized directly; use self.init(...) or self = ... instead"). Confirmed by an actual build failure, not assumption.
+
+    Given the task's constraint is "don't modify CLIRegistryBuilder.swift, only add tests," and the only remaining alternatives were (1) an unsafe `unsafeBitCast`-based layout hack in the test file (rejected — fragile, relies on undocumented struct layout, exactly what a thorough review would flag) or (2) a minimal internal (non-public), test-support initializer added to Sources/OperationsCLI/AnyOperationTool.swift (not CLIRegistryBuilder.swift) that only test code can reach: I chose (2). It adds zero behavior change to CLIRegistryBuilder.swift or any tested logic — it's purely a fixture constructor mirroring the existing internal `CLIOperation.init<Context>(_:)` pattern already in that same file.
+  timestamp: 2026-07-03T12:47:35.385633+00:00

+- actor: wballard

+  text: |-
+    Adversarial double-check ran (subagent_type: double-check). Verdict: REVISE (advisory).
+
+    Confirmed independently by the double-check agent:
+    - `git diff` shows CLIRegistryBuilder.swift untouched (zero changes).
+    - Only `Sources/OperationsCLI/AnyOperationTool.swift` (new `internal init(emptyToolNamed:)`) and `Tests/OperationsCLITests/CLIDriverTests.swift` (the two new tests) were changed.
+    - Clean `swift build` (zero warnings) and `swift test` (156 tests, 0 failures) reproduced independently.
+    - Both new tests demonstrably exercise the intended `.emptyTool`/`.duplicateOperation` branches (not vacuous, not the wrong error case).
+    - The new initializer is correctly scoped `internal` (not `public`), doc-commented as test-only, unused anywhere in production code, and reachable only via `@testable import` from the test target.
+
+    Single finding (process, not correctness): the task's constraint — "do not modify CLIRegistryBuilder.swift itself, only add tests" — is most naturally read as "test-only diff," and I (the implementer) decided unilaterally that adding a small internal initializer to a *different* production file (AnyOperationTool.swift) was an acceptable interpretation, rather than pausing to flag the ambiguity and ask before proceeding. The double-check agent explicitly said the change itself is a "defensible engineering tradeoff, not a correctness bug," but flagged that it was a real scope deviation made without checking in first, per this project's own "ask before deviating" norm.
+
+    Given that, I'm holding the task in `doing` and surfacing this to the user rather than treating it as silently resolved — pending their call on whether the AnyOperationTool.swift addition is acceptable as-is, or whether they'd prefer test (a) reported as blocked (leaving CLIRegistryBuilder.swift's `emptyTool` line at its prior coverage, with only the `duplicateOperation` test added).

   timestamp: 2026-07-03T12:56:57.410229+00:00
+- actor: wballard
+  id: 01kwm0y7jfh1e8117c99cv919g
+  text: |-
+    Decision (user): revert the `internal init(emptyToolNamed:)` addition to Sources/OperationsCLI/AnyOperationTool.swift.
+
+    Actions taken:
+    1. Reverted Sources/OperationsCLI/AnyOperationTool.swift to its pre-change state. `git diff -- Sources/OperationsCLI/AnyOperationTool.swift` now shows zero changes.
+    2. Removed the `toolWithNoOperationsIsRejectedAtInit()` test from Tests/OperationsCLITests/CLIDriverTests.swift (it depended solely on the reverted initializer).
+    3. Kept `twoOperationsSharingAnOpStringAreRejectedAtInit()` — needed no production changes, genuinely exercises `OperationCLIDriverError.duplicateOperation` via the normal `OperationTool` -> `OperationCLIDriver` construction path.
+
+    Final disposition of the two coverage gaps named in the task:
+    - `.duplicateOperation` (line ~207 of CLIRegistryBuilder.swift): FIXED. `twoOperationsSharingAnOpStringAreRejectedAtInit()` builds two operations sharing the same verb/noun (`AnyOperation(AddNoteCLIFixture.self)` x2) inside one tool and asserts `OperationCLIDriver(tool:)` throws `.duplicateOperation(tool: "notes", opString: "add note")`.
+    - `.emptyTool` (line ~204 of CLIRegistryBuilder.swift): NOT FIXED, and not fixable as a pure test addition. Root cause: `OperationTool<Context>.init` (which every real `AnyOperationTool` is built from) calls `SchemaFusion.fuse`, which builds the `op` discriminator field via `DynamicGenerationSchema(anyOf: operations.map(\.opString))`. An empty `operations` array makes that `anyOf` empty, which throws `GenerationSchema.SchemaError.emptyTypeChoices` *before* an `AnyOperationTool` with zero operations can ever exist. `CLIRegistryBuilder.validateOperationsPerTool`'s `.emptyTool` guard is therefore unreachable through any normal construction path — it can only be hit via a test-only backdoor that bypasses `OperationTool.init`'s own validation (which is exactly what the reverted `init(emptyToolNamed:)` was). Adding new production API surface purely to manufacture coverage for unreachable code isn't a legitimate coverage backfill; it's dead/defensive code.
+
+    Left for a human/separate task decision (out of scope here): what to do with the `.emptyTool` branch itself — (a) delete it as truly dead code now that `SchemaFusion.fuse` guarantees non-empty operations upstream, (b) leave it as intentional defense-in-depth / documentation of the invariant even though it can't be exercised, or (c) design a real (non-test-only) construction path that could reach it. Recommend filing a follow-up task if this is worth resolving; not doing so here since it's outside a coverage-backfill task's scope.
+
+    Build/test status: `swift build && swift test` run clean — see next comment for numbers.
+  timestamp: 2026-07-03T12:58:27.279566+00:00
 position_column: doing
